EDA

Descriptive statistics

The table below shows a statistical summary of the numerical features extracted from all sessions. Each row represents a feature, while each column describes an aspect of its distribution:

count: number of sessions with a valid value.
mean: average value of the feature.
std: standard deviation, indicating variability across sessions.
min: minimum observed value.
25%: first quartile (25% of sessions have values ≤ this value).
50%: median (50% of sessions have values ≤ this value).
75%: third quartile (75% of sessions have values ≤ this value).
max: maximum observed value.

This table helps identify outliers, understand session variability, and assess which features are useful for analysis or clustering.

count mean std min 25% 50% 75% max

Histograms

Histograms show the distribution of each numerical feature analyzed. Each plot represents a single feature and shows how its values are distributed across sessions:

X-axis: numerical values of the feature (e.g., duration, number of events, average time, etc.)
Y-axis: frequency, i.e. how many sessions fall into that value interval
Legend: name of the feature shown in the plot.

Histograms help identify outliers, concentrations, dispersion, and useful dataset patterns.

Correlation matrix

The correlation matrix shows the linear relationship between all numerical features analyzed. Each cell contains a value between -1 and +1, where:

+1: perfect positive correlation (the two features increase together);
0: no linear correlation;
-1: perfect negative correlation (one increases while the other decreases).

In the heatmap, more intense colors indicate stronger relationships: warm tones represent positive correlations, while cool tones indicate negative correlations. This analysis helps identify redundant features, recurring patterns, and possible relationships between variables that are useful for understanding the dataset and for later stages (feature selection and clustering).

Feature Selection

This section keeps only the features that are truly useful for analysis and clustering. The procedure is based on two main stages:

1) Variance Threshold: removes features with near-zero variance, i.e. variables that take almost the same value all the time and therefore add no useful information.

2) Correlation Threshold: removes one of two highly correlated features (correlation > 0.9), avoiding redundancy and preserving the more representative variable.

This selection reduces noise, removes redundancy, and improves the quality of subsequent analyses, especially clustering.

Retained features: session_duration, count_Teleport, count_Release, count_PlayVideo, count_Pause, count_Resume, count_Touch, unique_concepts_touched, concept_concept_IaaS, concept_concept_On-Premises, concept_concept_PaaS, concept_concept_SaaS, concept_concept_caratteristica, concept_concept_cloud_computing, concept_concept_fornitore_del_servizio, concept_concept_infrastruttura_cloud, concept_concept_livello_astratto, concept_concept_livello_fisico, concept_concept_modello_di_cloud, concept_concept_risorse_hardware, concept_concept_servizio, concept_concept_software

Drop log (variance/correlation)

reason feature correlated_with corr_value
correlation grab_release_count count_Release 1.000
correlation count_Grab count_Release 1.000
correlation touch_play_count count_Touch 0.987
correlation avg_touch_play_duration session_duration 0.948

Clustering

Clustering groups sessions automatically according to their behavioral characteristics. Each cluster represents a set of sessions that are similar to each other, identifying distinct patterns such as faster, more exploratory, more cautious, or more systematic users.

In this report, selected features are scaled with StandardScaler. Five algorithms are compared (KMeans, Agglomerative, Spectral, GMM, DBSCAN). For models that require a number of clusters, the following grid is tested: k = 3, 4, 5, 6, 7.

Best model selection criterion: results are ranked by:

  1. Silhouette score (higher is better)
  2. Number of clusters obtained (higher is better, when silhouette is tied)
  3. Davies–Bouldin index (lower is better)
The top-ranked model is labeled as the "Best model" and is used for cluster assignment and profile computation.

Best model: KMeans_k=3 (k=3)

What do PC1 and PC2 represent in the PCA plot?

The PCA plot shows sessions projected into two new dimensions called PC1 and PC2. These dimensions do not directly correspond to individual features (such as Pause, Touch, or Teleport), but are combinations of all features used for clustering.

PCA (Principal Component Analysis) is used to reduce data complexity while preserving as much information as possible about the differences between sessions, so that they can be visualized in a 2D plot.

PC1 (First Principal Component) is the dimension that explains most of the differences between sessions. It represents the main “direction” along which behaviors differ the most. Sessions far apart on PC1 have very different interaction strategies.

PC2 (Second Principal Component) is a second dimension, independent from PC1, that captures other important differences not explained by the first component. It helps distinguish sessions that may be similar on PC1 but different in other behavioral aspects.

How to read the plot:
• each point is a session;
• nearby points = similar sessions;
• distant points = different sessions;
• color indicates the assigned cluster.
If clusters are separated in PCA space, it means the model identified distinct behavioral groups.

PCA scatter — best

PCA scatter — next best 4

The following plots show the next 4 models in the ranking, to visually compare cluster separation.

GMM_full_k=3

KMeans_k=4

GMM_full_k=4

KMeans_k=6

Metrics (5 models × k=3..7)

This section compares the different algorithms using three standard metrics:

  • Silhouette Score: higher = more separated and compact clusters
  • Calinski-Harabasz Index: higher = more distant and compact clusters
  • Davies-Bouldin Index: lower = more distinct clusters
The best model is chosen according to the criterion described above.

model k silhouette calinski_harabasz davies_bouldin n_clusters

Sequence Analysis

Sequence Analysis examines the temporal order of events generated during sessions, helping understand how interaction evolves step by step. Unlike simple aggregate statistics, the goal here is to observe the flow of actions (Touch, PlayAnnotation, Grab, Release, Teleport, etc.) and the patterns with which they unfold over time.

The following are shown below:

  • the transition matrix, describing the probabilities of moving from one action to the next;
  • the most frequent bigrams and trigrams (sequences of 2 or 3 consecutive actions);
  • dwell time statistics, i.e. time intervals between consecutive events of the same action.

Sequence Pattern Detection

This section analyzes interaction patterns at the individual session level using metrics derived from event sequences (e.g., Touch, PlayAnnotation, Pause, Teleport). Instead of fixed thresholds, this version uses data-driven thresholds based on quantiles.

Each session receives:

  • effective_flags: positive patterns relative to the other sessions
  • problem_flags: potentially problematic patterns
  • pattern_score: sum (+1 for each effective flag, −1 for each problematic flag)
Flags also include the percentile, so the relative position in the distribution is clear.

Quick overview

  • Total analyzed sessions: 43
  • Linear sessions (score ≥ 2): 21
  • Intermediate sessions (0 ≤ score < 2): 14
  • Critical sessions (score < 0): 8

Pattern interpretation (flag guide)

The table below helps interpret the flags assigned to sessions. Each flag describes a behavior relative to the rest of the dataset.

Flag Quantitative meaning Behavioral interpretation
High Touch→Play consistency touch_to_play_rate in the highest percentiles (≈ ≥80th) Ordered workflow: after Touch, the user often starts PlayAnnotation.
Low Touch→Play consistency touch_to_play_rate in the lowest percentiles (≈ ≤20th) Scattered exploration or difficulty progressing after Touch.
Fast Touch→Play time median_touch_to_play_sec in the lowest percentiles High responsiveness: the user quickly moves from Touch to Play.
Slow Touch→Play time median_touch_to_play_sec in the highest percentiles Hesitation or slowdown before starting Play.
Limited Pause usage pause_per_min below the median / low percentiles Smooth session with few interruptions.
High Pause usage pause_per_min in the highest percentiles Fragmented or difficult session.
Moderate Teleport usage teleport_per_min below the median / low percentiles Controlled, non-chaotic navigation.
Frequent Teleport usage teleport_per_min in the highest percentiles Disordered exploration or continuous searching.
No long inactive periods idle_gaps_gt60 = 0 Continuously active session.
Many long inactive periods idle_gaps_gt60 in the highest percentiles Long pauses / temporary abandonment.
Stable post-Touch sequences entropy_next_after_touch low / low percentiles Actions after Touch are predictable and coherent.
Unstable post-Touch sequences entropy_next_after_touch high / high percentiles Irregular workflow, trial and error.

Behavioral archetypes (guide)

Archetypes are multi-label tags (max 2) assigned by combining sequence metrics. Thresholds are data-driven (quantiles/medians), therefore relative to your dataset.

Archetype Quantitative conditions (indicative) Behavioral interpretation
Fast content consumer High Touch→Play (≥80th) + Fast Touch→Play (≤20th) + Low Pause usage (≤ median) Frequently starts content and does so quickly, with few interruptions.
Reflective learner High Touch→Play (≥80th) + Slow Touch→Play (≥80th) + High Pause usage (≥80th) Interested in content but with longer decision times and more pause usage.
Focused explorer High Touch→Play (≥80th) + Low entropy (≤ median) + Low Teleport usage (≤ median) Coherent and predictable path: follows a logical thread, with limited spatial exploration.
Spatial explorer High Teleport usage (≥80th) + Low Touch→Play (≤20th) + Low Pause usage (≤ median) Moves a lot through space and rarely starts content; more “environmental” exploration.
Zapping / scattered exploration High Teleport usage (≥80th) + High entropy (≥80th) + Fast Touch→Play (≤20th) Frenetic / variable behavior: jumps across actions and locations with low stability.
Disoriented / struggling High entropy (≥80th) + High Pause usage (≥80th) + High Idle (≥80th and >0) Unstable sequences with many interruptions and/or stalls: possible friction or confusion.
Intermittent High Idle (≥80th and >0) + High Pause usage (≥80th) Session in bursts: activity alternates with long stops and restarts.
Passive observer Low Touch→Play (≤20th) + Low Teleport usage (≤ median) + Low entropy (≤ median) Limited and stable interaction: little exploration and few “strong” transitions.

All sessions are shown in sessionID order. Each panel includes cluster, pattern_score (colored badge), flags, sequence metrics, full counts, and timeline.
